coredata.dc:title: Quenching photoluminescence of Carbon Quantum Dots for detecting and tracking the release of Minocycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Rahal, Michel
Atassi, Yomen
Alghoraibi, Ibrahim
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the preparation of Carbon Quantum Dots via one-step microwave-assisted synthesis, to be employed for the determination of Minocycline antibiotic. Linear relationship was observed between photoluminescence quenching of CQDs and Minocycline concentration between 0.28–30.39 μM. UV–vis and PL Spectroscopy revealed that PL decrease arise from Inner Filter Effect. Selectivity studies were conducted to evaluate interference of various compounds on MC detection. In addition, Minocycline was loaded on Chitosan-grafted-oligomeric Lactic Acid microspheres, to be used as a drug carrier of Minocycline. Thereafter, CQDs were used to determine loading capacity, encapsulation efficiency, and cumulative drug release of Minocycline-loaded microspheres. Optimum samples showed loading capacity between 14.1–26.344 %, and encapsulation efficiency between 34.48–64.37%. CHS-g-OLA2 sample showed diminished burst release, and 50 % cumulative drug release during 4 days. Mathematical modelling showed that CHS-g-OLA2 sample follows Higuchi kinetic model, which describes the release of drug from swellable matrix by diffusion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Quantum Dots
Minocycline
IFE
Drug release

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g. technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support), but who do not meet the criteria for authorship, are named in the Acknowledgements and have given us their written permission to be named. If we have not included an Acknowledgements, then that indicates that we have not received substantial contributions from non-authors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CQDs in different magnifications.
UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL Spectra of CQD.
Relation between excitation and emission wavelength of CQD.
PL Intensity at different conditions: (a) temperature, (b) NaCl concentration, (c) pH variation, (d) time effect.
Raman Spectra of CQDs.
XPS Survey Spectra of CQDs.
High resolution peaks of (a) C1s, (b) N1s, (c) O1s, (d) S2p peaks.
Cells viability results of a) Caco-2 cells and b) HEK-293 Cells, for 24 and 48 h incubation at different concentrations.
PL intensity spectra of CQDs in presence of various concentrations of MC.
Linear regression between relative PL intensity and MC concentration.
Absorbance spectra of (a) MC, (b) CQD (c) MC andCQDs.
Tauc Plot of (a) CQD, (b) CQD and MC.
Parameters used in Eq. 7 projected on cuvette.
Schematic of (a) Observed quenching efficiency, (b) corrected quenching efficiency.
Photoluminescence intensity in the presence and absence of minocycline with (a) biomolecules, (b) Gentamycin, (c) Lidocaine, (d) Ions, (e) Agarose, (f) Starch.
Cumulative drug release of (a) CHS-g-OLA1, (b) CHS-g-OLA2, (c) CHS-g-OLA3.
Drug release models (a) Kors-Peppas, (b) Zero Order, (c) Higuchi, (d) First Order.
Comparison of different Tetracyclines detection methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (CQD: carbon quantum dots, GQDs: graphene quantum dots, TC: Tetracycline, OTC: Oxytetracycline, CTC: Chlorotetracycline, MC: Minocycline).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Parameters used to calculate IFE of MC on CQDs PL intensity.
Loading capacity and Encapsulation Efficiency results of different samples.

Mathematical models, equations and R2.

head.ce:title: Quenching photoluminescence of Carbon Quantum Dots for detecting and tracking the release of Minocycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology, Damascus, Syria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Institute for Applied Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Basic and Supporting Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Arab International University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Basic and Supporting Sciences, Syria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the preparation of Carbon Quantum Dots via one-step microwave-assisted synthesis, to be employed for the determination of Minocycline antibiotic. Linear relationship was observed between photoluminescence quenching of CQDs and Minocycline concentration between 0.28–30.39 μM. UV–vis and PL Spectroscopy revealed that PL decrease arise from Inner Filter Effect. Selectivity studies were conducted to evaluate interference of various compounds on MC detection. In addition, Minocycline was loaded on Chitosan-grafted-oligomeric Lactic Acid microspheres, to be used as a drug carrier of Minocycline. Thereafter, CQDs were used to determine loading capacity, encapsulation efficiency, and cumulative drug release of Minocycline-loaded microspheres. Optimum samples showed loading capacity between 14.1–26.344 %, and encapsulation efficiency between 34.48–64.37%. CHS-g-OLA2 sample showed diminished burst release, and 50 % cumulative drug release during 4 days. Mathematical modelling showed that CHS-g-OLA2 sample follows Higuchi kinetic model, which describes the release of drug from swellable matrix by diffusion.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines, family of bacteriostatic antibiotics that have broad-spectrum effect over wide range of gram-positive/negative bacteria, and against other microorganisms [1]. Minocycline (MC), a second-generation, semi-synthetic tetracycline analogue that has been used for 30 years. MC efficacy and safety is well proven on humans and animals [2]. It is approved from US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of several sexually transmitted diseases, rheumatoid arthritis [1], and for treatment of H. Pylori infection [3]. Besides conventional tablets, non-conventional drug carriers were employed to aim a controlled release of MC, as Nanoliposomes [4], PLGA nanoparticles [5,6], and Polyphosphoester based nanoparticles [7].

Numerous analytical methods have been used to detect MC, as capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) [8], HPLC-MS/MS [9] and HPLC-UV [10]. Though their sensitivity, These methods require expensive equipment, time consuming and well trained staff. Conversely, nanotechnology can offer fast, simple and low cost solutions, by employing the Photoluminescence (PL) of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). CQDs possess distinguished features as low or negligible photobleaching, PL stability overtime, low cost and available raw materials for synthesis, biocompatibility and low toxicity. With appropriate adaptation, CQDs demonstrated high ability to detect different types of analytes, as metal ions [11–13], antibiotics [14,15], nucleic acids [16], and others.

Herein, we report preparation of CQDs via microwave-assisted synthesis. CQDs employed for MC detection under different conditions, and determination of Loading Capacity, Encapsulation Efficiency, and Cumulative Drug Release of MC-loaded Chitosan-grafted-Oligomeric Lactic acid (CHS-g-OLA) microspheres. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first article that reports measurement of encapsulation and release of Minocycline using CQDs.

Chitosan (medium Mw) DA 80 %, n-Butanol, Hexane, Chloroform, Span 60, Methanol, Acetone, Glutaraldehyde were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Minocycline Hydrochloride was obtained from Alfares for Pharmaceutical Industry, Lactic Acid (90 %) was purchased from Riedel-de Haën, Urea, Uric Acid, Lactate, Cholesterol, Triglycerides standards were obtained from MEDICHEM MEA, Gentamycin and Lidocaine were purchased from Al-Dimas for Pharmaceutical Industry, Citric Acid, Thiourea, Sodium Hydroxide, Starch, Hydrochloride Acid and Sodium Chloride were purchased from Merck, Agarose and MTT reagent (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) were purchased from Genaxxon Bioscience.

XRD patterns were registered using X-ray diffractometer (Philips, X’pert) using CuKα (λ = 0.154 nm). UV–vis spectra were performed using JASCO, V-350 UV–vis spectrophotometer. PL Spectra were performed using JASCO FP-8300 Spectrofluorometer. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using Bruker Vector 22 spectrometer. Morphology was determined by Tescan Vega-II XMU5136 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), equipped with X-Ray detector (EDX) for elemental analysis. Raman Spectra was recorded using Horiba – T64000 instrument. XPS Spectra was performed on Phoibos MCD-5 instrument using Mg Kα X-rays as the excitation source, and data were analyzed using CasaXPS software. TEM images were acquired using FEI Tecnai F20. Particle Size was recorded on Mastersizer 2000. DSC Thermograms were recorded using SETARAM – DSC131 instrument. MTT assay performed on Epoch BioTek Microplate Reader.

1 g of Citric Acid, and 1 g of Thiourea were dissolved in 12 mL of distilled water, the solution transferred to a microwave oven for 10 min at power of 600 W. Black-brownish powder was formed and transferred to 10 mL of distilled water, dispersed well by ultrasonication, centrifuged on 6000 rpm for 60 min to separate dissolved carbon dots from large agglomerates, then placed in Vivaspin centrifuge columns and re-centrifuged (to separate remaining agglomerates), then heated in an oven at 45 °C under reduced pressure (0.01 MPa) for 24 h. Finally, a solid brownish powder was obtained.

The quantum yield of CQDs was calculated using the following equation [17]:
(1)
ϕ
C Q D
=
ϕ
S t
I
C Q D
I
s t
A
s t
A
C Q D
n
C Q D
2
n
s t
2
Where
ϕ
C Q D
is the quantum yield of carbon dots,
ϕ
S t
is the quantum yield of the standard (0.54 for Quinine Sulphate in 0.1 M of Sulfuric Acid),
I
C Q D
is the integrated emission intensity of carbon dots,
I
s t
is the integrated intensity of the standard,
A
s t
is standard absorbance,
A
C Q D
is CQD absorbance, and n is the refractive index of solvents (1.33 for water).

The selectivity of CQDs was evaluated in the absence and presence of MC, by testing possible effects of different types of molecules on the PL intensity. Biomolecules: Cholesterol (100 mg/dL), Uric Acid (8 mg/dL), Creatinine (2.5 mg/dL), Triglycerides (200 mg/dL), Total Protein (7 g/dL), Urea (50 mg/dL). Macromolecules: Agarose and Starch (0.5, 1, 1.5 mg/10 mL). Antibiotics: Lidocaine and Gentamicin (0.5, 1, 1.5 mg/10 mL). Ions: Ammonium, Barium, Nickel, Potassium, Sodium, Manganese, Phosphate, Citrate, and mixture (1 mM). Error bars represent standard deviation based on three measurements.

MTT assay was performed to evaluate the viability of two types of cell lines, Caco-2 cells (cancer cells) and HEK 293 cells (normal cells) in the presence of CQDs. Caco-2 cells were cultivated in DMEM medium and HEK cells were cultivated in RPMI medium, mediums contain trypsin and FBS (5%), with penicillin and streptomycin antibiotics, and 2 mM l-glutamine. Cells were placed in 96-well plate at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well. Different concentrations of CQDs were added to each well, then plate stored in an incubator at 37 °C with 95 % humidity and 5% CO2 for 24 and 48 h. After incubation, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and cells re-incubated for 4 h. Yellow formazan crystals in each well were dissolved using 150 μL of DMF, then the absorbance at 540 nm was taken for each well in triplicate. Cell viability calculated according to equation:
(2)
C e l l V i a b i l i t y
%
=
A s
-
A b
A c
-
A b
× 100
Where
A s
is the sample absorbance,
A c
is the absorbance of the negative control (cells without CQDs),
A b
is blank absorbance (reagents without cells).

The morphology and size of as prepared CQDs were determined by HR-TEM observation, as shown in Fig. 1 . in different magnifications. samples consist of nano-dots aggregations. Average size of CQDs 5 nm. Lattice spacing between planes was calculated 0.31 nm. These lattices are signature marker of graphitic sp2 structure [18].

Fig. 2 shows two electronic transitions in the UV–vis plot, one for π-π* transition at 230 nm, attributed to sp2 graphitic core electrons, and n-π* transition at 340 nm which results from edge functional groups electrons, that contain S, N and O atoms [19]. These atoms have higher electronegativity compared to carbon, thereby they inductively draw electrons out of π orbitals, a process facilitated by the high conjugation between carbon atoms [20]. The long absorption tail that extends to 550 nm is a result of the low energy transitions within the surface states, created by surface functional groups [18].

The emission spectrum shows blue emission centered at 416 nm under the excitation of 340 nm. Quantum yield was calculated in relative to Quinine Sulfate standard, and was 30.8 %. FWHM of spectrum was found to be 68.15 nm.

Fig. 3 shows excitation wavelength and PL intensity dependence, where the intensity was raised by raising excitation wavelength until reached maximum PL at 334 nm, thereafter the intensity decreased with higher excitation wavelengths.

PL Intensity was investigated in different conditions: thermal treatment, ionic strength, pH, and time. Fig. 4-a shows that CQDs maintained 95 % of their intensity when temperature reached 95 °C. Fig. 4-b shows no significant alteration of PL intensity when NaCl concentration reached 1.4 mM. Fig. 4-c shows PL intensity was varied when pH medium is changed, where the intensity has the higher value at neutral medium, while decreased in both basic and acidic mediums. Fig. 4-d shows that intensity of CQDs didn’t change with time over 1000 s, which reflects good stability of PL, where the higher deviation from the normal was 1.3 % and 1.28 % for upper and lower PL values.

Raman Spectroscopy is used to examine internal structure of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 , two typical bands located at 1348 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1, corresponded to D and G bands of carbon structures. D band refers to sp3 defects in carbon, while G band refers to sp2 bonded carbons [21]. The content of ordered and disordered graphitic structures can be estimated by using the ratio IG/ID [22], where IG and Id are intensities of G and D band, respectively. The ratio equals to 1.09, which implies that disordered and ordered regions are equal within CQDs structure.
Fig. 6 shows survey scan of CQDs, where four main peaks represent corresponding atoms: Oxygen (532.4 eV), Nitrogen (399.4 eV), Carbon (284.6 eV), and Sulfur (163.8 eV) [12].

Fig. 7 shows deconvolution of C1s peak into three peaks at 284.22 eV (CC) and 287.1 eV (CO, CN) [23]. N1s peak exhibited two peaks at 399.4 eV and 401.7 eV, revealing the presence of both pyrridinic N and pyrrolic N, where Pyridinic and Pyrrolic N are bonded to two neighboring carbon atoms at the edges and defect sites [24]. In addition, the low content of pyrrolic N bond may be due to the fact that pyrrolic nitrogen atom in the five sided ring is thermally unstable [25]. In addition, the high percentage of Pyridinic N may contributes by one electron to π system, which enhances the probability of electron excitation for same excitation energy, while Pyrrolic N may causes non-radiative transitions [26]. O1s spectrum showed two peaks at 532.3 eV (CO) and 530.4 eV (CO), and sulfur showed two distinct peaks, attributed to S2p3/2 at 163.6 eV and S2p1/2 at 165.1 eV, the S2p3/2 energy bonding is lower than elemental sulfur (164 eV), which suggests the C–S bonding in thiophene ring [27,28].

Wide range of CQDs concentrations (20, 60, 80, 120, 240 μg/mL) was applied on normal HEK 293 cells and caner Caco-2 cells lines to examine their cytotoxicity. From Fig. 8, cells viability didn’t exceed 88 % for 24 h incubation and 76 % for 48 h incubation for the highest concentration. Therefore CQDs exhibited no significant toxicity on both cell lines over 24 and 48 h, as shown in Fig. 8 , which indicates that CQDs are biocompatible.
Fig. 9 shows the change of PL intensity, as the concentration of MC increased, PL intensity was decreased regularly.

Fig. 10 shows relative PL intensity of CQDs as a function of MC concentration, where
I 0
and I are the intensities in the absence and presence of MC, respectively. Moreover, relative intensity
I 0
/ I displays linear relationship between 0.28–30.39 μM with correlation coefficient
R 2
= 0.995 , subsequently at higher MC concentrations a deviation from linearity was occurred (not shown). The linear regression equation can be expressed as:
(3)
I 0
I
= 0.02131
Q
+ 0.98928
Where
Q
is the quencher concentration.

TheLimit of Detection (LoD) was calculated by equation [29]:
(4)
L O D =
3 σ
m
Where σ is the standard deviation of blank, m is the slope.
LoD is 91 nM which possess good sensitivity, and Limit of Quantitation (LoQ = 3LoD) is 273 nM, which is close to the lowest concentration of the standard curve (288 nM).
Table 1 shows that CQDs are mostly employed to detect other Tetracyclines, where MC is often analysed by HPLC-UV and LC–MS/MS techniques.

Since each application require specific measurement range, the linear range between CQDs and MC is matching MC concentrations in human serum; since the oral dosage raises the concentration of MC in serum to 0.65 mg/L (1.4 μM) for 50 mg dose, and 2.2 mg/L (4.8 μM) for 150 mg/L dose, and 3.6 mg/L (7.87 μM) for 200 mg dose, while for intravenous dosage, MC concentration in serum is within 3–8.75 mg/L (6.65–19.1 μM) at 200 mg doses [30].

Several methods were conducted to investigate quenching mechanism between CQDs and MC. To examine static quenching mechanism, UV–vis method was employed. Fig. 11 Shows absorption spectrum of MC, CQD and mixture, where no shifting in absorbance wavelength for the mixture.

Additional investigation was conducted to detect any alterations in energy band gap of CQDs. Energy band gap was calculated from Tauc equation [37]:
(5)
α h v = B
( h v −
E g
)
n
Where α is the absorption coefficient, h v is the photon energy,
E g
is the bandgap energy, B is a proportionality constant, and n is a constant that depends whether the electronic transition is direct (n = 1/2), or indirect (n = 2). Absorption coefficient was calculated by using Beer-Lambert’s law:
(6)
α =
2.303 A
l
Where A is the absorbance from UV–vis spectra, l is the path length

Fig. 12 a shows that CQDs have two thresholds for two electronic transitions; the first transition at 2.4 eV, belongs to n - π* transition, while the second transition at 3.07 eV, belongs to π - π* electronic transition, while Fig. 12b shows only one electronic transition, attributed to π- π* transition at 3.09 eV, while n - π* transition is obscured by MC spectra. Since no change in the position of π - π* electronic transition, this indicates that no interaction between CQDs and MC, which confirms that no non-fluorescent complex has been formed, a prerequisite for static quenching [38].

Due to the absorbance overlap of CQDs (emitter) and MC (absorber), Inner Filter Effect (IFE) turn-off mechanism can be proposed. An important rule for IFE is there must be no interaction between the absorber and the emitter [39], which is confirmed in Tauc plot. To investigate IFE mechanism, Parker equation is used [40]:
(7)
F
c o r r
F
o b s
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 -
10
- d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 -
10
- s
A
e m
Where
F
o b s
is the observed PL intensity,
F
c o r r
is the corrected PL intensity after removing IFE from
F
o b s
,
A
e x
and
A
e m
are the absorbances at the maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength, s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm), g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.4 cm) and d is the cuvette width (1.00 cm), parameters are shown in Fig. 13 . The maximum value of the correction should not exceed 3, otherwise the correction is not convincing [41].

The measured and corrected quenching efficiencies were calculated using equation [42]:
(8)
E
%
=
1 -
I 0
I
× 100
Where
I 0
is the PL intensity in the absence of quencher, I is the PL intensity in the presence of quenching at each concentration. Eq. (8) is used to calculate
E
c o r r
: observed quenching efficiency and
E
c o r r
: corrected quenching efficiency (in the absence of IFE) (Fig. 13).

From Fig. 14 , it is shown that
E
o b s
increases when MC concentration increases, reaching 63.5 % efficiency at 30.39 μM, while for
E
c o r r
no quenching was observed.

Table 2 summarizes the impact of MC concentration on IFE, where the increase of the concentration is concurrent with
F
c o r r
F
o b s
and
E
o b s
increase, thereby the effect of IFE increases with the increase of MC concentration, which confirms the IFE mechanism [43–45].

Matrix Effect is defined as ‘The combined effect of all components of the sample other than the analyte on the measurement of the quantity [46]. The selectivity of MC detection was evaluated in the presence of biomolecules, ions, antibiotics and macromolecules.

For biomolecules, Fig. 15 a shows no significant reduction in PL intensity, and for the mixture the PL was reduced by 6.8 %, while in the presence of MC, PL reduction didn’t exceed 8%. Fig. 15b shows the effect of Agarose on CQDs PL intensity, the reduction was 4.8 %, while in the presence in MC the reduction was 6.2 %, while Fig. 15-c shows Starch effect, reduction was 17.2 %, while in the presence in MC the reduction was 14.8 %, for the highest concentration, Fig. 15d shows ions effect, where no significant change in PL intensity, only for the mixture, PL intensity was reduced by 12.8 %, while in the presence of MC, the reduction of PL intensity was about 15 % for the mixture. Fig. 15e shows the effect of Gentamicin on CQDs PL intensity, PL reduction was 6.9 %, while in the presence in MC the reduction was 3.2 %. Fig.15f shows the effect of Lidocaine on CQDs PL intensity, PL reduction was 1.7 % while in the presence in MC the reduction was reduced to 1.5 %. All mentioned results for highest concentration.

Eq. 3 was used to calculate the amount of released MC in the medium:
(3)
x = (
I 0
I
- 0.98928
0.0213
)

Preparation and characterization of MC-loaded microspheres are demonstrated in supporting data. Loading capacity (LC) and encapsulation efficiency (EE) of various MC-loaded microspheres are shown in Table 3 . It is shown that LC and EE are proportional to the molar ratio of the crosslinker glutaraldehyde (GTA) and free amine groups of Chitosan-grafted-oligomeric Lactic Acid (CHS-g-OLA); the higher the ratio, the lower LC and EE, considering that GTA reduces the drug release, where the surface of polymeric microspheres becomes less accessible to water penetration and hydration [47]. In addition, increased amounts of GTA will sink to the internal polymeric matrix and cross-link it, which make drug diffusion to the solution more harder. It was found before that the higher crosslinking degree leads to less swelling ability [48]. For next three samples, when the amount of MC was reduced, LC and EE were reduced.

Optimal values of LC and EE from literature are charted in Table 4 . It is shown that the higher EE is more desirable, ranged between 2–99.99 %, depending on the material properties and shape. Compared to literature, CHS-g-OLA2 has 45.92 % EE at 17.74 % LC which are similar to PLGA, a polymer known by hydrophilic properties.

CHS-g-OLA1−3 samples were utilized to investigate cumulative drug release (CDR) of MC-loaded microspheres. Fig. 16 shows cumulative drug release profiles of MC from three samples, as a function of GTA concentration. In general, all plots showed an initial rapid release, known as burst release, followed by slow and prolonged release. CHS-g-OLA1 showed burst release around 60 % of the drug release during 3 h, and 73 % during 4 days, while CHS-g-OLA2 showed 18 % burst release during 4 h, and 50 % during 4 days, and CHS-g-OLA3 showed 14 % burst release during 4 h, and 27.7 % during 4 days. As a consequence, burst release was reduced with increasing GTA concentration; in addition to cross-link microsphere surface, the internal structure was also cross-linked, which diminished its release ability. Noteworthy to mention that for last two formulas, burst release has close values, may be attributed to release from microsphere surface, followed by water penetration and swelling, and erosion of polymer matrix [53]. Since burst release cannot be easily controlled, it is preferable to reduce it as low as possible, because the initial high release rates may lead to drug concentrations near or above the toxic level in vivo [54].

To get insight into release mechanism, mathematical release models were applied on CHS-g-OLA2. Fig. 17 shows data fitted to Zero order model, Kors-Peppas model, First order model and Higuchi Model, and results charted in Table 5 . Model conformity with experimental data was chosen by higher R2 value. Microspheres drug release are better described by Higuchi diffusion Model, expressed by the equation [53]:
(9)
C t
=
K H
t
where it is used to describe the diffusion process based on Fick’s law for the release of drugs distributed within an insoluble and swellable matrix [55].

In summary, CQDs were used in to determine MC with a linear range between 0.2–30.39 μM, mechanism of quenching was IFE. MC selectivity was examined against different types of molecules. Loading Capacity (LC) and Encapsulation Efficiency (EE) of MC loaded-microspheres were determined. Results showed that microspheres have different LC and EE, depending on drug and GTA concentration. CHS-g-OLA2 sample showed 17.74 % LC and 48.79 % EE, and diminished burst release at 18 % during first 3 h, and prolonged release for 4 days. Mathematical modelling showed that the CHS-g-OLA2 sample is likely to follow Higuchi release model, a model that describes the drug release by diffusion from a swellable matrix.

Conflict of Interest and Authorship Conformation
o
All authors have participated in (a) conception and design, or analysis and interpretation of the data; (b) drafting the article or revising it critically for important intellectual content; and (c) approval of the final version.
o
There has been no significant financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome
o
This manuscript has not been submitted to, nor is under review at, another journal or other publishing venue.
o
The authors have no affiliation with any organization with a direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter discussed in the manuscript

All persons who meet authorship criteria are listed as authors, and all authors certify that they have participated sufficiently in the work to take public responsibility for the content, including participation in the concept, design, analysis, writing, or revision of the manuscript. Furthermore, each author certifies that this material or similar material has not been and will not be submitted to or published in any other publication before its appearance in the Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry.
Conception and design of study: M. Rahal, acquisition of data: M. Rahal, analysis and interpretation of data: M. Rahal,
Drafting the manuscript: M. Rahal, Y. Atassi, I. Alghoraibi, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content: M. Rahal, Y. Atassi
Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published : Y. Atassi, I. Alghoraibi

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g., technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support), but who do not meet the criteria for authorship, are named in the Acknowledgements and have given us their written permission to be named. If we have not included an Acknowledgements, then that indicates that we have not received substantial contributions from non-authors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113257.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
